Apple LADS image, extracted from Appendix B-5 (Apple monitor hex dump).
Load $79FD..$9071 (5749 bytes); entry $79FD -> JMP $82F5.
0 gaps, contiguous. NOTE: the Apple monitor format has NO per-line checksum
(unlike the C64/Atari MLX), so byte-level OCR cannot be auto-validated the way it
was for the others; 19 OCR'd ADDRESS fields were corrected by contiguous position.
This is the byte-exact Apple ORACLE. A labeled Apple SOURCE would be a full
decompile of B-5 (the book printed no Apple module listings, only the Defs).

DECOMPILED SOURCE: dist/apple-disk/lads.asm reassembles BYTE-EXACT to this image
(regen: tools/decompile.sh apple). Auto-labels (L####); a meaningful-label pass using
the C64 source as template is the next readability layer.

DISK IMAGE: lads.dsk (DOS 3.3, LADS B-file @ $79FD). Built to the documented format
and structurally validated (BAM/VTOC/catalog + file chain); not emulator-boot-tested here.

CANON vs STUDY: lads.asm (the decompile) is byte-exact and canonical. lads-printed-source.asm
is a STUDY transcribed from the book's Apple listings -- it assembles and is logically faithful
but NOT byte-exact, because the shipped object (B-5) carries tweaks the book never printed
(entry JMP $82F5, LDY #50 vs the shared EVAL's #48). Those are 1984 fixes/typos that only ever
lived in the object. The .bin is the artifact that must run; the printed-source asm is history.
